coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional solid-state electrochemiluminescence sensing platform based on poly(ethylenimine) capped N-doped carbon dots as novel co-reactant
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Libo
Liu, Dong
Mao, Hanping
You, Tianyan
coredata.dc:description:
A multifunctional solid-state electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensing platform based on poly(ethylenimine) (PEI) capped N-doped carbon dots (NCDs@PEI) as novel co-reactant of
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
nanosheets (RuNSs) was designed for the first time. In this route, RuNSs with large surface-to-volume ratio was used as luminophor, and NCDs@PEI, which was newly synthesized and modified in one step via a facile microwave-assisted method, was served as co-reactant. The as-prepared NCDs@PEI exhibited better catalytic effect to RuNSs than naked NCDs, due to the double enhancement contribution of NCDs and PEI to RuNSs. Simultaneously, reduced graphene oxide (rGO) was further introduced to facilitate the electron transfer and amplify the ECL signal. The developed ECL sensor (NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE) exhibited desired ECL emission, with about 69-fold enhancement of the ECL intensity in comparison with RuNSs/GCE. When used as ECL probe for dopamine detection, the prepared sensor showed high sensitivity in a wide linear range of 0.01–50μM with the detection limit of 10nM. Moreover, the sensing platform also suggested multifunctional application for detecting other compounds including bisphenol A, catechol and hydroquinone.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NCDs@PEI
Reduced graphene oxide
Ru(bpy)32+nanosheets
Solid-state ECL sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the solid-state ECL sensing platform
Application of the solid-state ECL sensing platform for bioanalysis
Characterization and ECL behavior of NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE
Application of the solid-state ECL sensing platform

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21222505 and 21475124 ) and the Priority Academic Program Development (PAPD) of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM, (B) XRD and (C) FT-IR spectra of NCDs@PEI; (D)-(F) high-resolution survey spectra of C1s, N1s and O1s of NCDs@PEI.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra, and (B) excitation and emission spectra of NCDs@PEI. Inset of A: NCDs@PEI solution under daylight (left) and 365nm UV light (right) illumination. (C) FL spectra of NCDs@PEI excited at different wavelengths in the range of 300–395nm. (D) Dependence of FL emission wavelength and intensity of NCDs@PEI on excitation wavelength.

(A) TEM and SAED (inset) of rGO. (B) TEM and SAED (inset) of NCDs@PEI-rGO. (C) Raman spectra of NCDs@PEI (inset), rGO and NCDs@PEI-rGO hybrid. (D) EIS of RuNSs/GCE (a), NCDs@PEI/RuNSs/GCE (b), and NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE (c) in 5mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] (1:1) solution containing of 0.5M KCl. (E) ECL-potential curves of different modified electrodes in 50mM pH 8.5 PBS. Conditions: scan rate, 0.1V/s; voltage, 800V.

(A) UV–vis and (B) FL spectra of NCDs@PEI (a), rGO (b),
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
(c) and mixture (d). (C) ECL spectrum of the NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE sensor in PBS solution (a) and FL spectrum of
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
solution (b). (D) Possible ECL mechanism of the NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE sensor.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms (CVs) (Top) and corresponding ECL-potential curves (Down) of the sensor in the absence (a, a′) and presence of 50μM DA (b, b′). Conditions: buffer, 50mMpH 8.5 PBS; scan rate, 0.1V/s; voltage, 800V. (B) Effect of DA, BPA, CT and HQ on the ECL intensity of the NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE ECL sensor in 50mMpH 8.5 PBS.
Fabrication process of the solid-state ECL biosensor .

head.ce:title: Multifunctional solid-state electrochemiluminescence sensing platform based on poly(ethylenimine) capped N-doped carbon dots as novel co-reactant

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering Institute of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Agricultural Equipment Engineering Institute of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin 130022, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A multifunctional solid-state electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensing platform based on poly(ethylenimine) (PEI) capped N-doped carbon dots (NCDs@PEI) as novel co-reactant of
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
nanosheets (RuNSs) was designed for the first time. In this route, RuNSs with large surface-to-volume ratio was used as luminophor, and NCDs@PEI, which was newly synthesized and modified in one step via a facile microwave-assisted method, was served as co-reactant. The as-prepared NCDs@PEI exhibited better catalytic effect to RuNSs than naked NCDs, due to the double enhancement contribution of NCDs and PEI to RuNSs. Simultaneously, reduced graphene oxide (rGO) was further introduced to facilitate the electron transfer and amplify the ECL signal. The developed ECL sensor (NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE) exhibited desired ECL emission, with about 69-fold enhancement of the ECL intensity in comparison with RuNSs/GCE. When used as ECL probe for dopamine detection, the prepared sensor showed high sensitivity in a wide linear range of 0.01–50μM with the detection limit of 10nM. Moreover, the sensing platform also suggested multifunctional application for detecting other compounds including bisphenol A, catechol and hydroquinone.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) process involves the electron-transfer reactions between luminous agents or between luminous agent and co-reactant (Richter, 2004). And it is becoming a more valuable detection technique in analytical chemistry and bio-sensing field due to the high sensitivity, low background signal, good stability and simplified instruments (Liu et al., 2015c, 2015a; Ding et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2014). In the recent works, inorganic nanomaterials with large surface-to-volume ratio, such as nanocrystals (Hesari et al., 2015b; Jiang et al., 2015b), semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) (Xia et al., 2015), graphene QDs (Dong et al., 2015; Zhao et al., 2015), conjugated polymer dots (Dai et al., 2015), carbon QDs (Wang et al., 2015b) and other inorganic nanocomposites (Jiang et al., 2015a; Gu et al., 2015; Hesari et al., 2015a), have been widely explored as new luminophores for ECL generation. However, some inorganic nanomaterials-based ECL emitters, especially the most studied Cd-containing QDs, possess inherent shortcomings including high toxicity, poor biocompatibility and low sensitivity (Dong et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2013). Moreover, although some carbon-based QDs have been proved to exhibit exciting ECL emission properties, they only show cathodic ECL signal (Yang et al., 2013; Chen et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2015b), which are not applicable to detect biomolecules that are easily to be oxidized and not suitable to fabricate biosensors.

Metal-organic complexes, especially classic
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
and its derivatives, not only can produce strong anodic ECL emission using analytes as co-reactants, but also can be covalently bonded to proper co-reactant to obtain self-enhanced ECL complexes with high luminous efficiency and stability (Zhuo et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2014; Hong et al., 2015). It is firmly believed when immobilizing
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
on a solid-state substrate or preparing into nanomaterials, the Ru (II) complexes-based ECL sensor is more superior to solution-phase ECL system, such as improved ECL efficiency and stability, reduced consumption of reagent and simplified design approach (Qian et al., 2014). Therefore, many efforts have been extensively devoted to establish regenerable solid-state ECL sensors and develop effective strategies for the immobilization of
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
(Li et al., 2012; Cao et al., 2013; He et al., 2013). For example, Li et al. (2012) prepared a novel
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
nanowires via a facile solvent evaporation route, and based on which a ECL biosensor with remarkable detection performance was developed. However, most of the effective co-reactants of
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
, such as tripropylamine (TPA) and 2-(dibutylamino)-ethanol(DBAE) (Liu et al., 2007a, 2007b) are toxic or volatile, which limit the application of
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
and affect the experimental accuracy. It has been widely accepted that compounds with abundant amine, especially tertiary amine can be served as effective co-reactant of
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
to obtain significantly enhanced ECL emission. Recently, Yuan's group implemented considerable effort on using poly(ethylenimine) (PEI) or polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimers, with abundant amine in their structures, as co-reactant or support to fabricate self-enhanced ECL biosensor or immunosensor (Zhuo et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2014; Hong et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2015a). This novel self-enhanced ECL system exhibits high ECL efficiency due to the shorter electron-transfer path and less energy loss.

Currently, it has been reported that carbon dots, a new member of luminescent materials with superior optical properties, low toxicity and good biocompatibility, is an eco-friendly and effective co-reactant of the anodic ECL of
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
(Long et al., 2014). And we also have illustrated that N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) can further enhance the ECL signal and improve the reproducibility and stability of
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
ECL system (Li et al., 2015b).

In the present work, in order to further improve the sensitivity, a novel co-reactant, PEI capped NCDs (NCDs@PEI) composite was firstly prepared via a facile microwave-assisted method, which smartly combined the double enhancement contribution of PEI and NCDs to the anodic ECL of RuNSs. The as-prepared NCDs@PEI exhibited low toxicity and high catalytic effect to RuNSs. Meanwhile, reduced graphene oxide (rGO) was introduced to accelerate the electron transfer rate and amplify the ECL signal. The fabricated ECL sensing platform (NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE) exhibited desired ECL intensity in comparison with RuNSs/GCE and good detection performance for analyzing some diphenol compounds.

Tris(2,2-bipyridyl) dichlororuthenium(II) hexahydrate (Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O), branched poly(ethylenimine) (PEI, Mw=25kDa), catechol and Nafion solution (5wt%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Citric acid and urea were provided by Beijing Chemical Reagents Factory and GEN-VIEW SCIENTIFIC INC., respectively. Dopamine and hydroquinone were offered by Alfa Aesar. Bisphenol A was provided by Aladdin. Dopamine hydrochloride injection was purchased from local hospital. Reduced graphene oxide (rGO) synthesized according to Hummer's method was purchased from Nanjing Xianfeng Nano Co. and used directly without any treatment. Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, KCl, K3Fe(CN)6, K4Fe(CN)6, ethanol, acetone, acetonitrile and n-propanol were of analytical grade, which were used as received without further purification.

Structure and morphology of the as-prepared carbon dots were characterized using a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and selected area electron diffraction (SAED), which was operated on a TECNAI F20 field-emission TEM at an acceleration voltage of 200kV. A D8 ADVANCE (Bruker AXS, Germany) X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.5406Å) was used to record the X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra. Fourier transformed infrared spectra (FT-IR) results were obtained from a VERTEX 70 FTIR. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by A Thermo ESCALAB 250 instrument, coupled with Al Kα radiation (VG Scientific, UK). A Renishaw Raman microscope (model RM2000) with a laser excitation wavelength of 514.5nm was used to record Raman spectrum. UV–visible adsorption spectra (UV–vis) and fluorescence spectra (FL) were tested on a UV mini 1240 and a LS 55 fluorescence spectrometer, respectively. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was taken on an AUTOLAB PGSTAT302N workstation.

Electrochemical (EC) experiments were performed on a CHI832 electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Apparatus Inc., China), and ECL signals were recorded simultaneously using a model MPI-E ECL analyzer (Xi'An Remax Electronic Science and Technology Co. Ltd., China) with a voltage of 800V supplied to the photomultiplier tubes (PMT). All experiments were carried out with a standard three-electrode system. Glass carbon electrode (GCE, 3mm) modified with different materials were used as working electrode, an Ag/AgCl (saturated KCL) as reference electrode and a Pt wire as counter electrode.

The photoluminescent N-doped carbon dots modified with branched PEI (NCDs@PEI) was synthesized by a one-step facile route of microwave assisted method. Briefly, 3g citric acid, 3g urea and 6g PEI was thoroughly dissolved in 10mL ultrapure water by vigorous stirring for 0.5h to forming a homogeneous solution in a common beaker. Next, the solution was heated for different time intervals in a domestic microwave oven (800W). When cooled down to room temperature, the solid products were re-dissolved in deionized water, and isolated from the large carbon residual via centrifugation with 12,000rpm for 20min. Then the opaque supernatant solution was dialyzed against pure water using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 50kDa for three days to remove the excessive precursors. Finally, the solution was concentrated to dry at 60°C under vacuum to obtained powdered product. As a control, NCDs was also prepared via the same route in the absence of PEI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the solid-state ECL sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para:
Fabrication process of the solid-state ECL sensing platform is shown in Scheme 1: first, bare GCE was polished using alumina powders with average particle diameters of 0.3 and 0.05µm, respectively, and then ultrasonically washed with distill water, ethanol, acetone and distill water successively. After dried in air, the cleaned GCE was modified with
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
acetonitrile and n-propanol (5:1, v/v) solution similar to previous report (Li et al. 2012). With the organic solvent gradually evaporated at the room temperature,
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
molecules concentrated, nucleated and grew to form RuNSs. Then the RuNSs-modified GCE (RuNSs/GCE) was further coated by the mixture of NCDs@PEI and rGO aqueous solution, and finally covered with 0.5% Nafion film to obtain the ECL sensing platform, abbreviated as NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE. For comparison, RuNSs/GCE, rGO/RuNSs/GCE and NCDs@PEI/RuNSs/GCE were prepared via the similar route.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the solid-state ECL sensing platform for bioanalysis
ce:section.ce:para: All ECL measurements were performed at room temperature and in aqueous solution. For this, phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was utilized as blank and analytes stock solution were added to give various concentrations. The ECL signals could be recorded as cycling the potential from +0.5 to +1.3V (vs. Ag/AgCl). The quenched ECL efficiency was defined as I/I0, where I0 and I represented the ECL intensity of the sensor in PBS solution in the absence and presence of analytes, respectively.

Dopamine injection was firstly diluted by deionized water to 1mM. Then, certain concentrated standard solutions of dopamine were added into buffer solution containing 10μM dopamine, and the found concentration and recoveries were estimated.

In order to obtain NCDs@PEI with desired catalytic properties for RuNSs anodic ECL, effect of PEI content was firstly evaluated by adjusting its content in the reaction precursors. It was found that, when the mass ratio of citric acid/urea/PEI was 1:1:2, the highest ECL intensity was obtained (Fig. S1). The resultant NCDs@PEI exhibited significant water-solubility, that no noticeable precipitation in the homogeneous phase is observed at room temperature for two months.

The structure and morphology of the NCDs@PEI were investigated by TEM and XRD. It can be seen from TEM image ( Fig. 1A) that quasi-spherical NCDs@PEI are well-dispersed and have a narrow size distribution in the range of 13–20nm, with the average diameter of 16.0nm. The larger diameter of NCDs@PEI than that of naked NCDs (3.0±0.5nm, Fig. S2A) should be indicative of successful modification of PEI on the surface of NCDs. The conclusion can be confirmed by the change of zeta potential of NCDs from −6.72 to 3.49mV before and after functionalized by PEI. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. 1B) for NCDs@PEI displays a broad and low-intensity diffraction peak at 2θ=20.6°, corresponding to the interlayer spacing of 0.43nm, suggesting the poor crystallization of the NCDs@PEI (Liu et al., 2012). Additionally, it is notable to find that the interlayer spacing of NCDs@PEI (0.43nm) is larger than that of NCDs (0.36nm, Fig. S2B). This should be ascribed to the introduction of abundant passivated groups on the surface of NCDs@PEI (Hu et al., 2014).

The functional groups on the surface of bare and PEI-modified NCDs were characterized by FT-IR. For the bare NCDs (Fig. S2C), the characteristic absorption peaks for the stretching vibration of C=O at 1716 and 1586cm−1, C–N or C–O bonds at 1189cm−1 and N–H or O–H at 3200cm−1are observed. However, after capped with PEI (Fig. 1C), except characteristic absorption peaks of N–H or O–H (3362cm−1) and C=O stretching vibration (1676cm−1) from NCDs, several characteristic peaks of PEI, such as 2940 and 2872cm−1accounted for CH2asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration (Liu et al., 2015b), 1562cm−1 for N–H bond, 1448cm−1 for CH2 stretching vibration deformation are also observed (Liu et al., 2012; Shen et al., 2013), demonstrating the presence of PEI on NCDs.

XPS were performed to measure the chemical composition of NCDs@PEI. Three peaks located at around 285.0, 399.0 and 531.0eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s, were observed. The C1s core level spectrum (Fig. 1D) can be deconvoluted into five components located at 284.0eV, 284.6eV, 285.1eV, 285.9eV and 287.4eV, which are associated with C=C, C–C, C–N, C–O and C=N/C=O bonds, respectively. N1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) of NCDs@PEI reveals the existence of C–N–C (398.1eV), N–(C)3 (398.8eV), and N–H (399.5eV) bonds. The O1s peaks (Fig. 1F) located at 530.4eV and 531.9eV are assigned to O in the states of C=O and C–O–C/C–O–H, respectively.

Optical performance of the prepared NCDs@PEI was investigated by UV–vis and fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy. Insets of Fig. 2A show that NCDs@PEI aqueous solution is yellowish under daylight, while emits blue luminescence under excitation of UV light (365nm), which is similar to NCDs (Insets of Fig. S2D). As shown in Fig. S2D, the bare NCDs exhibits two absorption peaks at 340nm and 404nm, assigning to the π-π conjugate structures of NCDs. However, it can be clearly observed that the characteristic peak at 404nm disappears upon the capping of PEI (Fig. 2A). Furthermore, FL spectra (Fig. 2B) illustrate that NCDs@PEI exhibits maximum excitation and emission wavelength at 355–395nm and 475nm, respectively. In comparison with bare NCDs (Fig. S2E and F) or previously reported NCDs@PEI (Liu et al., 2012; Shen et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2015b), the as-prepared NCDs@PEI shows two main differences: firstly, about 41 and 57nm blue-shifted excitation and emission wavelength; secondly, nearly excitation-independent emission wavelength and FL intensity (Fig. 2C and D) in the range of 355–395nm. The unique FL features of NCDs@PEI are attributed to the uniform surface state of NCDs@PEI upon the introduction of PEI into the matrix (Liu et al., 2015b; Li et al., 2015a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and ECL behavior of NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. S1, as a novel co-reactant, the as-prepared NCDs@PEI smartly combine the double enhancement of NCDs and PEI for
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
, leading to 3.6-fold increase of the ECL intensity in comparison with bare NCDs. However, the relatively poor conductivity of NCDs@PEI would restrict the electron transfer when it was used to modify working electrode and fabricate ECL biosensor (Hu et al., 2015). Herein, rGO was further introduced to improve the conductivity of the co-reactant. The mixture of rGO and NCDs@PEI were firstly characterized by TEM. Individual rGO exhibits two-dimensional planar structure ( Fig. 3A), and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) result (Inset of Fig. 3A) shows relatively good crystallization of rGO. While for the mixture of NCDs@PEI-rGO (Fig. 3B), NCDs@PEI are uniformly anchored on the surface of rGO or dispersed around rGO, leading to the random aggregation and overlap of the crumpled rGO sheets. Furthermore, SAED of NCDs@PEI-rGO (Inset of Fig. 3B) shows that diffraction ring of rGO are blurred upon the addition of NCDs@PEI, which can be ascribed to the amorphous structure of NCDs@PEI. Raman spectra (Fig. 3C) further suggest the amorphous structure of NCDs@PEI-rGO hybrid. Individual NCDs@PEI exhibits no Raman peak (Inset of Fig. 3C), but both rGO and NCDs@PEI-rGO hybrid feature distinct G band and D band at ca. 1360 and 1575cm−1. The decreased peak intensity and increased ratio of D and G band intensity (I D/I G) of NCDs@PEI-rGO (0.88) than rGO (0.8) might be attributable to the surface defects in NCDs@PEI structure (Hu et al., 2015).

Sequentially, using NCDs@PEI as co-reactant and rGO as signal amplification agent, a novel solid-state ECL sensing platform base on
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
nanosheets (Fig. S3) (NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE) was designed for the first time. As a control, ECL sensors based on different materials were prepared, and investigated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques. As can be seen from Fig. 3D, electron-transfer resistance of RuNSs/GCE is 1.4kΩ (a), which dramatically increases to 6.2kΩ with the introduction of NCDs@PEI (b) due to the low conductivity of NCDs@PEI from the capping of PEI. However, the NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE exhibits a quite low electron-transfer resistance of 169Ω upon the addition of rGO into the co-reactant system (c), indicating a fast charge transfer rate of the established ECL sensing platform, resulting from the excellent conductivity of rGO.

As previously reported, the fast charge transfer can induce enhanced ECL signal (Guo and Dong, 2004), therefore, the ECL behavior of NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE sensor in PBS buffer was investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) technology in detail. Fig. 3E shows that RuNSs/GCE emits relatively weak ECL signal (~100a.u.) with large background noise (a), which should be attributed to the absence of co-reactant and slow charge transfer rate, respectively. After further modified with conductive rGO, background noise of the ECL sensor decrease, but the ECL intensity increases unobviously (b). In contrast, NCDs@PEI/RuNSs/GCE exhibits sharply improved ECL signal (~1800a.u.) due to the effective enhancement effect of NCDs@PEI on RuNSs anodic ECL (c). And as predicted, the novel ECL sensor using NCDs@PEI as co-reactant and rGO as signal amplification agent shows the strongest ECL emission (d) (~6900a.u.), with about 69-fold and 3.8-fold enhancement of the ECL intensity in comparison with RuNSs/GCE and NCDs@PEI/RuNSs/GCE, respectively.

UV–vis, FL and ECL spectrometry were performed to investigate the ECL mechanism of NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE system. Fig. 4A illustrates that NCDs@PEI solution exhibits a characteristic peak at ca. 340nm (a), while rGO shows no absorption peak (b). For separated
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
, two adsorption peaks at 284 and 455nm, ascribed to the π-π* transition and metal-to-ligand charge transfer of
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
are observed, respectively (c). However, UV–vis spectrum of the mixture solution of NCDs@PEI, rGO and
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
(d and Fig. S4A) indicates that each individuals preserve their UV–vis absorption features in the mixture, suggesting no direct chemical reaction occurs. Similarly, FL spectrum of the mixture solution also exhibit the emission features of NCDs@PEI (490nm) and
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
(611nm), but no emission peak for rGO (Fig. 4B, d and Fig. S4B–D), since rGO itself does not possess the FL emission property (Fig. 4B, b). FL results further confirm that NCDs@PEI, rGO and
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
would not react. Interestingly, as can be seen from Fig. 4C, ECL spectrum of the NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE(a) are consistent well with the FL spectrum of
Ru ( bpy )
3
2 +
(b), indicating that RuNSs, NCDs@PEI and rGO served as luminophor, co-reactant and amplification reagent, respectively. Therefore, according to the present study and previous reports (Long, et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2013; Zhuo et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2014; Hong et al., 2015), related mechanism of the NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE system might be described as follows (Fig. 4D):

Where the RuNSs(II) are electro-oxidized on the surface of GCE to form RuNSs(III) (a). And simultaneously NCDs@PEI is also oxidized to produce [NCDs@PEI] ·+ radical, which then lose two proton to generate a strongly reducing intermediate radical ([NCDs@PEI] · ) (b). The [NCDs@PEI] · could react with the RuNSs (III) to produce excited-state RuNSs (II)* (c), which are capable to undergo orange ECL emission (d). And during the whole reaction process, the conductive rGO accelerates the charge transfer rate on the modified GCE surface and amplifies the ECL signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the solid-state ECL sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the potential applications of the developed ECL sensing platform for bioanalysis, dopamine (DA), an important forerunner associated with many diseases, was selected as a model molecule. As can be seen from Fig. 5A, the NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE exhibits strong EC (a) and ECL (a′) signal in 50mM PBS. However, the current and ECL intensity significantly decrease upon the addition of DA (b and b′). On the basis of the quenched ECL by DA, DA was quantitatively detected under the optium conditions (Fig. S5A–C) and a wide linear range of 0.01–50μM with the following equation: I0/I=0.525 logCDA+2.562 (R2=0.983) (Fig. S5D) was obtained. The detection limit for DA was 10nM, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) for nine successive scanning of one measurement and for six separated measurements is 4.64% (Inset of Fig. S5D) and 6.14%, demonstrating good stability and reproducibility of the sensing platform. Moreover, analysis of DA in DA hydrochloride injection by standard addition method suggested the developed platform possess high accuracy in practical applications.

Moreover, it can be clearly observed from Fig. 5B that analytes such as bisphenol A (BPA), catechol (CT) and hydroquinone (HQ) also lead to obviously reduced ECL response of the sensing platform, suggesting multifunctional application of the developed ECL sensing platform based on the energy-transfer reaction between oxidation products (benzoquinone species) of analytes and excited state RuNSs(II)* (McCall et al., 1999; Liu et al., 2007a, 2007b).

In summary, a multifunctional solid-state RuNSs ECL sensing platform using NCDs@PEI as novel co-reactant and rGO as signal amplification agent was developed. In this route, NCDs@PEI was prepared and modified in one step via a facile microwave-assisted method for the first time. Since the surface of the composite was coated by abundant amine groups, the NCDs@PEI exhibited better catalytic effect to luminophor than naked NCDs. Moreover, rGO was introduced to accelerate electron transfer rate and amplify the ECL signal. The fabricated solid-state NCDs@PEI-rGO/RuNSs/GCE ECL sensor exhibited desired detection performance in a wide linear range (0.01–50μM) and low detection limit (10nM) for analyzing dopamine, indicating its promising application in bioanalysis. Moreover, the sensing platform also suggested multifunctional application in many fields.
The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21222505 and 21475124) and the Priority Academic Program Development (PAPD) of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.
